title: How to Use Automatic Writing to Access Your Intuition
meta_description: Unlock your intuition with automatic writing. No special gifts needed. Just a pen, paper, and willingness to let your hand move. Start now.
og_title: How to Use Automatic Writing to Access Your Intuition
og_description: Unlock your intuition with automatic writing. No special gifts needed. Just a pen, paper, and willingness to let your hand move. Start now.
body:
You've Been Writing Messages You Didn't Know Were Yours

Let me guess. You've had that moment where you're staring at a blank page, and suddenly words start pouring out that don't sound like you. Maybe it was a journal entry that got weirdly specific about something you couldn't have known. Maybe it was a text to a friend where you wrote something so wise you thought, "Wait, did I just say that?"

That's not a glitch in your brain. That's your intuition using your hand to get your attention. And automatic writing is the most direct way I know to turn that occasional glitch into a full-on conversation with your deeper self.

Here's the thing - you don't need to be a psychic medium or have any special gifts to do this. You just need a pen, paper, and the willingness to let your hand move without your brain getting in the way. That's it. Right?!

I've been teaching this for years, and the people who struggle with it aren't the ones who "can't hear their intuition." They're the ones who won't stop editing. They write three words, think "that's stupid," and stop. They're so used to filtering themselves that they can't let the raw stuff through.

But you can learn. And when you do, it changes everything.

What Automatic Writing Actually Is (And Isn't)

Let's clear something up right now. Automatic writing isn't about channeling spirits or getting messages from the dead. Can that happen? Sure, for some people. But that's not what we're doing here. We're talking about bypassing your conscious mind to access your own intuitive intelligence - the part of you that knows things your rational brain can't explain.

Think of it this way. Your conscious mind is like a security guard at a gate. It's trained to check everything, filter it, and decide what's allowed through. Your intuition is the person on the other side of the gate trying to hand you a package. Automatic writing is when you tell the security guard to take a coffee break and let the packages come through unfiltered.

You've already experienced this, by the way. Every time you've had a "gut feeling" about something and ignored it, only to find out later you were right - that's your intuition trying to get through. Automatic writing just gives it a direct line.

"Your intuition doesn't speak in complete sentences with perfect grammar. It speaks in fragments, images, and feelings. Automatic writing is just giving it permission to use your hand instead of your stomach."

Why Your Brain Will Fight You On This

I'm not going to lie to you - your brain is going to hate this at first. Your rational mind has spent your whole life being the one in charge. It's built entire systems of logic and reason to keep you safe. And now you're telling it to sit down and shut up while your hand does something that makes no logical sense.

It's going to rebel.

You'll get thoughts like:

  • "This is stupid, I'm just making this up."
  • "I'm not a real psychic, what am I even doing?"
  • "This doesn't make any sense, I should stop."
  • "I'm just writing what I already know."

All of that is normal. All of that is the security guard screaming from the break room. Your job is not to believe those thoughts. Your job is to keep your hand moving and let the words come out anyway.

Does that land? Because this is where most people quit. They get three sentences in, the inner critic pipes up, and they put the pen down. Don't do that. Keep going. The first three sentences are warm-up. The real stuff comes after you push through the noise.

How to Set Up Your Practice (The Simple Way)

You don't need a special altar, crystals, or candles. You don't need to be in a perfect meditative state. You just need a few things that actually work:

What You'll Need

  • A pen that writes smoothly - no scratchy, skipping pens. You want zero friction between your hand and the page.
  • Paper that feels good to write on. A notebook you like. Nothing precious, nothing you're afraid to "ruin."
  • A quiet space where you won't be interrupted for 15-20 minutes.
  • That's it. Seriously.

If you want to get fancy, you can light a candle or put on some ambient music. But don't let the lack of a perfect setup become an excuse. You can do this in your car, on your lunch break, or sitting on your bathroom floor if that's where you can get five minutes of quiet. Know what I mean?

The Step-by-Step Process

Here's exactly what I want you to do. Follow these steps, and don't skip any of them:

Step 1: Set Your Intention

Before you put pen to paper, say this out loud or silently to yourself: "I'm going to let my intuition speak through my hand. I'm not going to judge or edit anything that comes through. I'm just going to write."

That's it. You don't need a fancy prayer or invocation. You're just telling your system what's about to happen.

Step 2: Ask a Question

Automatic writing works better when you give it something to work with. Ask a question that matters to you. Not "What should I have for dinner?" but something that has emotional weight. Try questions like:

  • "What do I need to know right now?"
  • "What's blocking me from moving forward?"
  • "What's something I'm not seeing about this situation?"
  • "What does my intuition want me to understand?"

Write the question at the top of the page. Then put your pen on the line below and start writing the answer. Don't think about it. Just write whatever comes.

Step 3: Keep Your Hand Moving

This is the hardest part. Your brain is going to try to slow you down, edit you, and make you sound smarter. Don't let it. Write as fast as you can without thinking. If you get stuck, write "I don't know what to write" over and over until something else comes. It will.

Your hand should be moving faster than your thoughts. That's the whole trick. When your hand outruns your brain, your intuition has room to slip in.

Step 4: Don't Read It Yet

When you're done, close the notebook. Don't read what you wrote. Put it away for at least a few hours, ideally overnight. Reading it fresh gives your rational brain a chance to analyze and dismiss it. Let it sit. Come back to it later when you can read it like it was written by someone else.

What to Do When Nothing Happens

Sometimes you sit down to write and... nothing. Your hand stays still. Your mind is blank. You feel like an idiot sitting there with a pen.

That's fine. That's normal. Here's what to do:

  • Write anyway. Start writing nonsense. "I am sitting here writing nonsense and nothing is happening and I feel stupid and this is dumb and I don't know what to say." Keep going. Eventually, something shifts.
  • Switch hands. If you're right-handed, try writing with your left hand (or vice versa). This bypasses your trained writing patterns and forces your brain to let go of control.
  • Change your question. Sometimes the question you asked is too vague or too loaded. Ask something simpler. "What's one thing I need to hear today?"
  • Walk away. If you've been sitting there for ten minutes and nothing is happening, stop. Go do something else. Come back tomorrow. Forcing it never works.

The first few times, you might get nothing but static. That's okay. You're teaching your system a new way to communicate. It takes practice. Don't judge the results. Just keep showing up.

How to Read What You've Written

When you come back to your writing, read it like you're reading a letter from a friend. Don't analyze every word. Don't look for hidden meanings. Just notice what stands out.

Here's what to look for:

  • Phrases that surprise you. If you read something and think "I didn't know I thought that" - that's gold. That's your intuition talking.
  • Recurring themes. If the same idea shows up in different ways, pay attention. Your intuition is trying to get a message through.
  • Emotional reactions. Notice what makes you feel something. A sentence that makes you tear up or feel a rush of recognition is significant.
  • Practical advice. Sometimes your intuition gives you very specific, actionable guidance. Don't dismiss it because it seems simple.

If you want to go deeper, try this: take one sentence from your automatic writing and use it as a prompt for more automatic writing. Ask "What does this mean?" and write the answer. You can keep going deeper and deeper.

Common Mistakes That Kill the Practice

I've seen people struggle with automatic writing for years. Here are the most common mistakes and how to avoid them:

Mistake 1: Trying Too Hard

You can't force intuition. It's like trying to force yourself to fall asleep. The harder you try, the less it works. Relax. Treat it like a game. Let your hand be silly. Let it write things that don't make sense. The pressure to be "deep" is what blocks you.

Mistake 2: Judging the Output

You write something and immediately think "That's stupid" or "That doesn't make sense." Stop. You're killing the flow. Your intuition doesn't communicate in neat, logical packages. It communicates in fragments, metaphors, and sometimes complete nonsense that only makes sense later. Let it be messy.

Mistake 3: Expecting Immediate Results

This is a practice. Like meditation or exercise, you don't get the full benefit on day one. You build the muscle over time. The first few sessions might feel like nothing. Keep going. The connection gets stronger with repetition.

Mistake 4: Over-Interpreting

Don't turn every sentence into a symbol you need to decode. Sometimes a sentence is just a sentence. Let the meaning reveal itself naturally instead of forcing it.

Taking It Deeper: Questions That Actually Work

Once you've gotten comfortable with the basics, you can start asking more specific questions. Here are some that tend to open up deep material:

  • "What am I not letting myself feel?"
  • "What's the story I've been telling myself that isn't true?"
  • "What would my life look like if I wasn't afraid?"
  • "What do I need to heal right now?"
  • "What's the next step I'm avoiding?"
  • "What does my younger self need me to know?"

These questions work because they bypass your rational defenses. Your brain can't give a pat answer to "What am I not letting myself feel?" It has to go deeper.

Resources to Support Your Practice

If you want to go deeper into this work, here are some books that have helped countless people I've worked with:

For a practical, no-nonsense guide to developing your intuitive abilities, check out See on Amazon (paid link). It's a solid foundation for anyone just starting out.

If you're looking for a more structured approach to automatic writing and journaling, See on Amazon (paid link) offers exercises and prompts that can help you push through blocks.

For those who want to understand the psychology behind why we block our intuition, See on Amazon (paid link) is a deep dive into the inner critic and how to work with it.

And if you're ready to integrate automatic writing into a broader spiritual practice, See on Amazon (paid link) offers a gentle, compassionate approach that pairs beautifully with this work.

The Real Point of All This

Here's what I want you to take away. Automatic writing isn't about getting messages from some external source. It's about remembering that you already know more than you think you do. You've been carrying wisdom your whole life that you haven't given yourself permission to access.

Your intuition isn't something you need to develop. It's something you need to stop blocking. Automatic writing is just a tool to get out of your own way.

The first time you write something that makes you cry because it's so true, you'll understand. The first time you get an answer to a question you've been wrestling with for months, you'll feel it. The first time you realize that the voice on the page knows you better than you know yourself - that's when everything shifts.

So here's what I want you to do. Right now. Get a pen and paper. Write down one question that matters to you. Then write for five minutes without stopping. Don't think. Don't edit. Just let it come.

The only way to learn this is to do it. And you're ready. You've been ready. You just needed permission.

If you're curious about where your intuitive blocks are and what's keeping you from accessing your deeper knowing, take the free assessment at intuitivesawaken.com/assessment. It'll show you exactly what's in the way and how to start clearing it.
